narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 63.9% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your class standing in Year 3, where you placed in the first decile—far below the fifth-decile average among those who passed the bar. That low ranking stood out as the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signals challenges in sustaining high performance over time. Your Year 1 decile of 2 also contributed noticeably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles compared to the typical sixth-decile standing of successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success since it's closer to the 3.24 average of those who passed, though not enough to offset the other influences. While your upper-middle family income aligns well with the majority of passers and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for them, these didn't carry enough weight to change the overall outlook, especially given your LSAT score of 27, which lags well behind the 37 average for bar success. In essence, the model saw your deciles as the top drivers of risk, unlike accepted applicants who typically show consistent top-half rankings throughout undergrad and law school prep. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your upper-level grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and perhaps seeking mentorship from bar-passed alumni who started from similar deciles. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools like ours. You're clearly driven to pursue this path full-time, and with focused improvements, the model—and admissions—could see you differently next time. Keep pushing; many have turned early setbacks into bar triumphs.
